The three gateway modules in web/src/server/ follow two different naming conventions: rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts use the *-gateway.ts suffix while mcp-deps.ts uses a *-deps.ts suffix. A developer adding a fourth gateway has two conflicting naming precedents to choose from — standardize on one suffix via a naming rule in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md before a fourth gateway is added.

The dual fragility criterion (cohesion < 0.7 AND coupling > 0.3 simultaneously) is met by exactly two zones: task-usage-tracking (0.63/0.38) and websocket-realtime-layer (0.5/0.5). All other zones are either high-cohesion or low-coupling, but not both-fragile. These two zones should be the first structural targets in any web package refactor.

Across all four identified intra-package call cycles (rex: 337 calls, sourcevision: 757 calls, llm-client: 88 calls, plus the web inter-zone cycle), none are surfaced by the per-zone cohesion/coupling metrics — zone-level metrics have a consistent blind spot for intra-zone cycles. A complementary cycle-detection check at the sub-zone call-graph level would catch all four of these cases that the current tooling misses.

Zone naming in the web package is split between two incompatible conventions: package-prefix names (web-viewer, web-server, web-unit, web-landing) and content-descriptor names (dom-performance-monitor, logo). A contributor cannot determine zone ownership from zone ID alone without knowing which convention applies. Establish a single taxonomy — either all zones use package-prefix (web-dom-monitor, web-logo) or all use content descriptors — and enforce it in the architecture-policy test.

The cli-e2e-tests zone is the only zone where test files use .js instead of .ts. This is the correct choice for black-box CLI invocation tests, but the convention is implicit and undocumented. Any new top-level test suite added to tests/ will face an undocumented choice of language, and the wrong choice (.ts for e2e, .js for unit tests) would introduce inconsistency without triggering any existing lint or policy check.

The architecture-policy test is the sole mechanism enforcing structural constraints across the entire codebase, yet it lives inside a behavioral test zone (cli-e2e-tests) and has no dedicated CI step. If cli-e2e tests are skipped, throttled, or made environment-dependent, all structural enforcement silently degrades. The policy test warrants its own CI job with explicit pass/fail visibility separate from behavioral coverage.

Across all zones, the only naming inconsistency pattern is in test file naming: zone utility tests mirror the utility filename (dom-performance-monitor.test.ts) while hooks in the same zone lack a corresponding test file. Codifying a zone-completeness rule (every exported public symbol in a zone must have a corresponding test file) would make coverage gaps structurally visible rather than discoverable only by manual audit.

Establish a formal CI gate on web package structural health: track bidirectional cycle count (currently 2) and minimum zone cohesion (currently 0.5 for web-unit). Any PR that increases cycle count or decreases minimum cohesion below its current value should require explicit architectural review. This turns the implicit 'web is the sole risk surface' property into an enforced invariant.

The call graph sub-zone naming scheme ('packages-rex:prd-analysis-core') and zone ID naming scheme ('rex-runtime-state') are incompatible identifier formats for the same codebase. Align sub-zone IDs to the descriptive convention used by top-level zones, or document the translation rule explicitly in CONTEXT.md so that consumers of both analysis outputs can cross-reference without manual interpretation.

'web-unit' (production components) and 'packages-llm-client:unit' (test suite) both use 'unit' in their zone identifiers but mean opposite things. Establish and document a project-wide convention: 'unit' in a zone name means test suite only, and production utility modules use 'shared', 'utils', or 'common'. Apply this retroactively to 'web-unit' to eliminate the ambiguity.

Add *-gateway.ts as the canonical suffix for all gateway modules in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md and rename mcp-deps.ts to follow the convention. With three gateways today and the project growing, inconsistent naming will cause a fourth gateway to be placed with the wrong suffix by default — standardize now while the surface is small.

The dual fragility criterion (cohesion AND coupling both non-trivial) is the most actionable structural metric not currently tracked in any CI or dashboard view. Adding a health check that flags zones where cohesion < 0.7 AND coupling > 0.3 simultaneously would have caught both task-usage-tracking and websocket-realtime-layer as first-class issues rather than leaving them as buried zone-level observations.

The call graph reveals intra-package bidirectional cycles in rex, sourcevision, AND llm-client — three of the four non-web packages. This is a systemic pattern indicating that all packages were developed without enforcing DAG ordering within their internal sub-modules. A per-package internal cycle check (e.g. madge --circular per package) added to CI would prevent new intra-package cycles from forming silently.

Adopt a single zone naming taxonomy enforced in the architecture-policy test: either all zones use a package-prefix (web-*, rex-*, hench-*) or all use content-descriptors, but not both. The current mix (web-viewer vs dom-performance-monitor in the same package) makes zone ownership unreadable from IDs alone.

The architecture-policy test is the only structural enforcement mechanism in the project but runs without a dedicated CI step and shares failure signal with CLI behavioral tests. Promote it to a standalone CI job that runs on every PR independently of the e2e suite so that structural regressions are always visible regardless of e2e suite status.

The implicit convention that e2e test files use .js and package tests use .ts is undocumented and unenforced. Add a rule to the architecture-policy test that rejects .ts files under tests/e2e/ and .js files under packages/*/tests/ to make the convention explicit and machine-checked.

SYSTEMIC ENFORCEMENT GAP: Findings 7, 8, 9, and 12 all independently arrive at the same root cause — structural invariants exist as code (architecture-policy test, gateway pattern, cycle freedom) but none are enforced as dedicated CI gates. This is not four separate suggestions; it is one organizational failure manifesting in four places. The correct fix is a single CI job addition, not four separate work items.

NAMING FRAGMENTATION IS PERVASIVE AND SYSTEMIC: Findings 4, 5, 6, and 10 each identify a separate namespace that uses an ad-hoc convention — zone IDs, gateway file suffixes, sub-zone identifiers, and package scope prefixes. Taken together they indicate the project lacks a single authoritative naming document that all conventions defer to. PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md exists but does not cover zone IDs or sub-zone identifiers; CONTEXT.md covers neither. One consolidated naming spec would resolve all four findings.

WEB PACKAGE IS THE SOLE STRUCTURAL RISK SURFACE: Findings 0, 3, 7, 11, 14, 15, 19, 20, 22, 23 — 10 of 24 findings — are scoped exclusively to the web package. No other package contributes more than 2 findings. This concentration is not coincidental: web is the only package that imports from all domain layers (rex, sourcevision, hench), making it structurally exposed by design. The risk is real but contained; the correct mitigation is the CI gate from finding 7, not decomposition.

DEAD EXPORT ACCUMULATION PATTERN: Findings 14–20 identify 7 potentially unused exports across 6 files in hench, sourcevision, and web. The exports span polling restart state, module loading, glob matching, branch lifecycle detection, PRD resolvers, cache instances, and node listeners. This scatter pattern — unused symbols across many unrelated files — is characteristic of incremental API churn without a corresponding cleanup pass, not a design problem in any individual module.

CONTRADICTION — FAN-IN SEVERITY INVERSION: Finding 2 rated 'warning' for schema/index.ts at 22 callers; finding 13 rated 'info' for walkTree at 29 callers. The higher-fan-in symbol received lower severity. Both are central modules used as intended. Severity 2 should be downgraded to 'info' to restore consistency (captured in severityUpdates).

INTRA-PACKAGE CYCLES ARE A CROSS-PACKAGE PATTERN: Finding 9 documents bidirectional cycles in rex, sourcevision, and llm-client. Finding 0 documents bidirectional coupling between two web sub-zones. Together these show that cycle tolerance is an ambient project norm, not an isolated incident. A single madge --circular sweep added to CI (one command, one threshold) would enforce DAG ordering project-wide without requiring per-package fixes first.

Consolidate findings 4, 5, 6, and 10 into a single work item: author a NAMING.md (or extend PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md) that specifies: (a) zone ID convention (content-descriptor vs package-prefix — pick one), (b) sub-zone ID format aligned with zone IDs, (c) gateway module suffix (*-gateway.ts canonical), (d) 'unit' reserved for test zones only. This one document eliminates all four naming findings.

Consolidate findings 7, 8, and 9 into a single CI work item: add one GitHub Actions job named 'structural-health' that runs (1) the existing architecture-policy test in isolation, (2) madge --circular per package, and (3) a zone-metrics check that fails if any zone has cohesion < 0.7 AND coupling > 0.3. This single job enforces the invariants described across three separate findings.

The architecture enforces inter-package DAG ordering (four-tier hierarchy, gateway pattern) but applies no equivalent discipline to intra-package module ordering. This asymmetry means cross-package structure is auditable but internal package structure is opaque. Adding `madge --circular` per package in CI closes the gap without changing any existing code.

Seven potentially unused exports (findings 14–20) across 6 files suggest no periodic dead-code audit exists. A project-wide pass using the existing call-graph data (already computed by sourcevision) to flag zero-incoming-call exports would surface all of these in one step and establish a repeatable process.

The web package's structural complexity (bidirectional coupling, lowest cohesion zone at 0.5, two god-function candidates, 142-call module pair, multiple dead exports) is fully explained by its architectural role: it is the only package that integrates all other packages. This is not an accident of implementation — it is load-bearing complexity. The correct response is metric tracking with enforced thresholds (finding 7), not decomposition.

Inter-package structure is rigorously governed (gateway pattern, four-tier DAG, documented conventions) but intra-package module ordering has no equivalent enforcement. This asymmetry means cross-package boundaries are auditable while internal package structure is opaque — the single largest structural governance gap in the project.

All web-package metric findings (bidirectional coupling, lower cohesion, god-function hook, dead exports, tightly coupled module pair) share a single architectural cause: web is the sole integration layer for all other packages. Treating these as individual defects would be incorrect; the correct frame is metric-tracked load-bearing complexity with enforced thresholds.

The seven dead-export findings across six files are a symptom of one missing process: no CI gate on zero-incoming-call public exports. Sourcevision already computes the required data during analysis; the gap is enforcement, not detection.

The two highest fan-in modules — packages/rex/src/schema/index.ts (22 callers) and packages/rex/src/core/tree.ts:walkTree (29 callers) — define the project's highest-risk change surface. Neither is a design flaw; both are expected hub types. Together they are the two files most deserving of focused regression test coverage.

The two god-function findings have fundamentally different risk profiles: a CLI dispatch main orchestrating 48 commands is structurally mandated by its role, whereas a React hook aggregating 39 callees is not — only usePRDActions warrants active monitoring for further growth.

Global finding 0 (bidirectional web↔web-viewer coupling, severity: warning) directly contradicts global finding 16 (LLM analysis: web's structural complexity is load-bearing and intentional, correct response is metric tracking). Finding 16 is correct: 6 total crossings from a deliberate integration package is expected behavior, not a defect. The coupling should be tracked via enforced thresholds as recommended in finding 5, not treated as a warning-level structural problem.

Global finding 2 (fan-in hotspot: packages/rex/src/schema/index.ts flagged as warning) contradicts the barrel-export convention documented in CLAUDE.md ('Public API: src/public.ts → exports["."] in package.json — all 5 packages follow this'). Schema index files are explicitly designed to be the canonical import point; 22 callers is the intended outcome of the pattern, not a hotspot defect. The finding correctly identifies that changes here have wide impact, but warning severity is inappropriate for a module whose high fan-in is a deliberate design consequence.

The structural-health CI job proposed in global finding 5 (madge --circular + zone-metrics check) should add a third sub-check: a fan-in threshold (e.g., > 30 callers) applied exclusively to non-index, non-barrel files. This correctly flags future walkTree-class hotspots while exempting schema/index.ts and similar barrel exports that are designed for wide import. The distinction — barrel vs non-barrel — can be approximated by excluding files named index.ts or public.ts.

Seven potentially unused exports across six files (packages/web/src/viewer/polling/polling-restart.ts: 3 exports; packages/web/src/viewer/loader.ts: 2 exports; packages/hench/src/shared/glob.ts: simpleGlobMatch; packages/sourcevision/src/analyzers/branch-work-filter.ts: detectBranchLifecycle; packages/sourcevision/src/cli/commands/prd-epic-resolver.ts: resolveWorkedEpicTitlesForRange; packages/web/src/server/routes-token-usage.ts: getAggregationCacheInstance) share one root cause: no CI gate enforces that non-test public exports remain reachable. Sourcevision's call-graph already tracks this data; adding a CI step that fails on zero-incoming-call exports in non-test, non-barrel files would surface and prevent all of these without new tooling.

The naming-convention gap (finding 4) and the structural-health CI gap (finding 5) are the only two findings that, if addressed, would prevent recurrence of multiple other findings: naming conventions would prevent new zone-ID inconsistencies; CI enforcement would prevent new dead exports, new circular dependencies, and new cohesion/coupling regressions. Prioritizing these two meta-level work items yields higher long-term leverage than fixing any individual instance finding.
